Cedar Cliff Honors-AP-College Summer Work

As we head into the 2025-2026 school year, I am both humbled and honored to introduce myself as the new principal of Cedar Cliff High School. For me, this serves as an example of our promise to all Cedar Cliff Students - “You can always come home,” -  as I began my career at Cedar Cliff as a Social Studies Teacher in 2000. I cannot wait to make connections with your students and be part of the Cedar Cliff community!
Mrs. Jennifer S. Post#WestShoreProudPrincipal, Cedar Cliff High School

Attendance Information
                    
                            Absence Verification
Cedar Cliff Honors-AP-College Summer Work
Parents shall submit the appropriate form no less than five (5) school days in advance of the proposed trip. Failure to follow this procedure will result in denial of the educational trip request.
Parents shall submit the appropriate form no less than five (5) school days in advance of the proposed trip. Failure to follow this procedure will result in denial of the educational trip request.

A family having children enrolled in more than one building who will be participating in the trip shall submit one (1) request form. The form shall list the name of each child for whom permission is sought to participate in the trip and shall be submitted to the principal serving the youngest child's building. The principal receiving the form will take responsibility for notifying the principal(s) of the other building(s) and will consult with them on the appropriate response. A copy of the form will be forwarded to each principal.

A family having children enrolled in more than one building who will be participating in the trip shall submit one (1) request form. The form shall list the name of each child for whom permission is sought to participate in the trip and shall be submitted to the principal serving the youngest child's building. The principal receiving the form will take responsibility for notifying the principal(s) of the other building(s) and will consult with them on the appropriate response. A copy of the form will be forwarded to each principal.
The principal receiving the request will notify the parents in a timely manner of the decision to approve or disapprove the trip.
The principal receiving the request will notify the parents in a timely manner of the decision to approve or disapprove the trip.

The cumulative number of days for educational trips that will be considered for approval during a given year shall not exceed five (5) school days per child. The cumulative number of days that will be considered for approval of college visitations also shall not exceed five (5) school days per child.

The cumulative number of days for educational trips that will be considered for approval during a given year shall not exceed five (5) school days per child. The cumulative number of days that will be considered for approval of college visitations also shall not exceed five (5) school days per child.
Educational Trips and College visitations will not be approved for the first five (5) school days of the school year, nor the final ten (10) days of each school term.
Educational Trips and College visitations will not be approved for the first five (5) school days of the school year, nor the final ten (10) days of each school term.
Requests for educational trips, including college visitations, will be considered in terms of student absence guidelines delineated in Board Policy 204.
Requests for educational trips, including college visitations, will be considered in terms of student absence guidelines delineated in Board Policy 204.
Approved trips will be recorded as excused absences and will not be considered as days of attendance for consideration of perfect attendance.
Approved trips will be recorded as excused absences and will not be considered as days of attendance for consideration of perfect attendance.
Shopping, hunting, fishing, visits to relatives, etc.
Events or places which could be visited outside of school time because they are nearby and/or scheduled for substantial hours beyond the school day.

Trips to educational sites for which a  previous request has been approved. Site in this instance refers to a specific place; for example, it would be possible to schedule several trips to New York City without visiting the same sites.

Trips for more than five (5) school days duration (cumulative within a given school year). Absences beyond five (5) days will be recorded as "unlawful" or "unexcused", as appropriate, and subject to provisions outlined in Attendance Policy 204.
Accompanying the family to locate a new home due to a planned move.
Trips taking place during the time period in which District testing or final examinations are being administered.

Cedar Cliff Honors-AP-College Summer Work

Let's continue to celebrate the details and spotlight great student behavior at Cedar Cliff! Teachers, please remember to hand out DETAIL tickets to students who are demonstrating great Cedar Cliff behaviors. Let’s continue recognizing students who show respect, responsibility, and positive actions throughout the school day. Thank you for helping us shine a spotlight on the great things our students are doing!

PROM will be held on 5/2/26 at the Penn Harris Hotel from 6:00 – 10:00. Tickets are $65(cash or check made payable to CCHS) and will be sold from Monday April 20th through April 24th during the 4s in the concourse area. If you are planning on bringing a guest who does not attend CC, you must have a guest form approved prior to purchasing tickets.  Guest passes can be picked up in the main office or outside of room 132 and 116. No refunds will be given after 4/24 and absolutely NO tickets will be sold after 4/24.

In celebration of Cedar Cliff’s first-ever Facility Dog, we invite everyone to Mr. D’s 2nd Birthday Ice Cream Fundraiser on Tuesday, April 21st during all lunches.  The ice cream trucks will be here with a selection of dairy and non-dairy pre-packaged ice cream treats for purchase starting at $5.50.  A portion of the money raised will go towards the care of our new facility dog, Mr. D.  For everyone who buys ice cream, their name will be entered into a drawing for a special prize, with the one ultimate winner getting a personal special meet and greet with Mr. D. You might even see yourself on the TV news that day!

In JV baseball…The young Colts came out slugging and stayed hot beating Muhlenberg 10-0 in 5 innings.  Mason Miller started on the mound going 4 strong innings and plated the walk off run in the 5th in much less dramatic fashion as the previous game.  The Colts offensive was led by Eli Edwards 3 Hits, 3 Runs scored and Harrison Brown 3 Hits, 2 RBI’s and a Run scored.  Mason Anastasi chipped in with 2 Hits, an RBI and a Run scored along with Mason Miller’s 2 Hits and 2 RBI’s.  The Colts travel to Cumberland Valley on Monday.
The American Sign Language Club will be meeting today after school in Mr Murphy’s room, room 131. Come and invite friends to learn new signs and have fun!

Word on the street is, “Girls dig guys with blood donation bandages.”  Get yours on April 17th.  Every donation can help save up to three lives. Whether you're a first-time donor or a regular, your contribution truly matters.  Donation is open to students, staff, and community members.  You must be seventeen to donate or sixteen with parental consent. Consent forms can be picked up in the JROTC offices located in the library.  Sign up today, get free cookies and be a hero in someone’s story. Let’s come together and show the power of our school community!

Join Mr. P at Pinchot State Park this Saturday, April 18, 2026, starting at 9:00 a.m. for the CMS Semi-Annual Fishing Tournament at the Conewago Day Use Area. No advance sign-up required. The tournament will take place rain or shine. Come out for a chance to win a $50 Bass Pro Shops gift card. Even if you don’t catch a lunker, there are additional prizes—including a Peewee prize. For more information, see Mr. P in the Tech-ed shop.

Good Morning.  (Just a reminder that  JROTC will be holding their Annual Blood Drive this Friday). If you are donating Blood, Please………. remember to bring school ID Card and Parent Consent Form.  Also… Thursday night, drink plenty of water, eat breakfast get a good night rest.  Thank you for saving lives with Central Pennsylvania Blood Bank.
Math & Snacks is today in the cafeteria from 3-4pm. Hope to see you there!

Get Ready for “Guess the Theme” Day! . Our teachers are planning something fun... and a little mysterious! This Friday, April 17th, teachers will dress in the same themed outfits, but it’s up to YOU to figure out the theme! How to Play: Look closely at what your teachers are wearing this Friday, take your best guess at the theme, Write your guess down and drop it in the guess box located in the office by the start of Period 6 on Friday, The drawing will take place before the afternoon announcements. The theme and winner will be announced during Friday's afternoon announcements at 2:35 PM. Prizes can be picked up in the office at the end of the school day. Start thinking about what the theme could be for this Friday!

Hey Colts, after an intense interview with Mrs. Post, Decker has returned as Mr. D!  He will need some time to adjust back to school life, and home life so please give him some space this week as you see him about.  Mr. D has sent a letter to all his fellow teachers that they can read in class. He looks forward to Fistbump Friday to show his school spirit and stay tuned tomorrow for details about his upcoming birthday.

PROM will be held on 5/2/26 at the Penn Harris Hotel from 6:00 – 10:00. No refunds will be given after 4/24 and absolutely NO tickets will be sold after 4/24.

Your Cedar Cliff Colts Boys' Tennis team wrapped up their Mid Penn Conference season with a resounding 5-0 victory over the Warriors of Gettysburg. Adam "Hit-Man" Hintze blanked his #1 Singles opponent 6-0, 6-0 in a 30-minute mauling. Likewise, Mason "Madman Mongo" Cordaro crushed his foe 6-1, 6-2 at #2 Singles. At #3 Singles, Rojan "The Road Warrior" Tiwari took down his antagonist in a 6-2, 5-7, 6-1 best two out of three set duel. Then, Drake "Big Daddy Diesel" Dietterick teamed up with Noah "The American Nightmare" Artz at #2 Doubles while body slamming their adversaries 6-0, 6-1.  However, the match of the night belonged to Lincoln "The Missing Link" Govelovich and Dylan "The Dynamite Kid" Candioto as they survived a three set, two-hour marathon by winning a third-set tiebreaker with a 10-6 RKO out of nowhere.  Congratulations Colts and best of luck at next week's Mid Penns.

A reminder to students who are competing in the 30-2010 competition today, you need to report to the library at 7:55 AM this morning. The competition will last from 8:00 to approximately 10:00. Good luck! If you have questions, see Mrs. Kamps.

Join Mr. P at Pinchot State Park this Saturday, April 18, 2026, starting at 9:00 a.m. for the CMS Semi-Annual Fishing Tournament at the Conewago Day Use Area. No advance sign-up required. The tournament will take place rain or shine. Come out for a chance to win a $50 Bass Pro Shops gift card. Even if you don’t catch a lunker, there are additional prizes—including a Peewee prize. For more information, see Mr. P in the Tech-ed shop.

On Monday…The young Colts continued their hot streak defeating Northeastern 10-1 on the road last evening.  Roman Davidson was outstanding on the mound going 7 innings allow one run on 5 hits earning the victory.  Offensively the Colts were led by Keegan Maley with 2 Hits, 2 RBI’s and 2 Runs scored and Owen Whitaker with 2 Hits, 2 Runs scored and an RBI.  The Colts are back home on Tuesday hosting Altoona.

Tired of the cold? Brighten your day and support the Class of 2028! We’re selling flowers for planting to bring the freshness of spring to your home! Online orders will be directly delivered to your door for $6.99 shipping fee. See the QR codes on the posters around the school or go to cchs.fpfundraising.com to place your order. Thank you for your support!

Do you want to donate for a good cause, and get out of class to do it? Then sign up and donate for the cedar cliff blood drive! Not only do you get food if you donate, but it goes to a good cause that could save hundreds of lives. The blood drive will take place on Friday, April 17, and it will run from 8 A.M to 2:30 P.M. You must be seventeen to donate or sixteen with parental consent. Consent forms can be picked up in the JROTC offices located in the library.  More information will be available at the lunchroom booth on the 7th, 8th, and 10th of April.  All participants will receive a free T-Shirt.

Cedar Cliff Colts JV baseball had a double header on Tuesday night. Game 1.The young colts took a hard fought loss in Game 1 hosting Mifflin 6-2.  Harrison Brown started on the mound for the Colts giving up 4 runs, 2 Earned, in 3 innings of work giving way to Colby Watts, Ryerson Gregorio and Parker Denezza who were able to keep the Huskies within striking distance.  Offensively the Colts were led by Harrison Brown with 2 hits and an RBI, Roman Davidson with 2 Hits and a Run Scored and Luke Gelencser chipping in a Hit an RBI and a Run Scored. Game 2. The young colts came out on fire scoring 4 runs in the first inning only to see the Huskies storm back in the bottom of the inning with 5 of their own in a tough 7-5 loss in Game 2.  Keegan Maley started on the mound for the Colts giving up 5 runs in 1 inning of work giving way to Roman Davidson and Cam Regis who were able to keep the Huskies at bay.  Offensively the Colts were led by Mason Miller 2 Hits a Run and an RBI, Ethan Fleishman 2 Hits a Run and 2 RBI’s and Harrison Brown with a hit and 2 Runs Scored. The Colts were on the road Wednesday against Chambersburg.

The young colts defeated Chambersburg 2-0 for their first win of the season!  Owen Whitaker was nothing less than outstanding on the mound going 6 innings allowing just one hit.  Mason Anastasi closed it out for the colts in the 7th.  Offensively the Colts were led by Mason Miller who had the colts lone hit, an RBI single.  Harrison Brown chipped in with a SAC Fly scoring Sullivan Laudenslager.  The colts are back at home Friday against Manheim Township.

Congratulations to your Cedar Cliff Colts Boys' Tennis team as they took down both the Panthers of Central Dauphin East and the Thundering Herd of Carlisle in consecutive matches recently.  Adam "The Bomb" Hintze led the charge by outdueling both his opponents in straight sets.  Mason "The Bricklayer" Cordaro leapt, spun, and lobbed his way to a three-set comeback win as Rojan "The Robot" Tiwari did the same with their wins being key to sending "The Herd" scrambling away in defeat.  Meanwhile, the doubles tandem of Drake "The Snake" Dietterick and Noah "Patron of the" Artz skinned their Panther opponents with yet another three-set gem.  Finally, Dylan "The Dynamo" Candioto and Lincoln "Too Tall" Govelovich paddled the Panthers in straight sets to assure another team victory. Afterwards, the newly formed District Doubles duo of Hintze and Govelovich secured all important wins as Mid Penn seeding approaches.  Way to go, gentlemen.  Remember sports fans, in the immortal words of Snoopy, "It doesn't matter if you win or lose...until you lose."

Congratulations to senior Jadyn Arroyo! Jadyn has received national recognition for his creative writing! Scholastic Writing has awarded Jadyn a National Silver Medal for his poem entitled "Para el Niño de Esas Fotos."  Jadyn's writing was selected to receive national recognition out of over 335,000 student writing and art submissions. Scholastic Writing is proud to publish Jadyn's poem in the online galleries at artandwriting.org. Jadyn's name will also be listed in Yearbook 2026, a companion to the annual art and writing anthologies that feature selections of works by National Medalists. Congratulations on this phenomenal achievement and recognition, Jadyn!

World Down syndrome Day is on Saturday, March 21st.  This year’s theme for World Down Syndrome Day is "Together Against Loneliness,” emphasizing that everyone deserves friendship, community, and opportunities. Loneliness isn't just about being alone but about a lack of true connection, a problem tackled through collective action from individuals, families, organizations, and governments.  You can help support real inclusivity by inviting others to join your conversations, lunch table, group activities, and games.  It takes a village!

On this day in 1933, the Senate confirmed FDR's appointment of Frances Perkins as Secretary of Labor, making her the first woman to hold a cabinet position. She would play a central role in establishing New Deal policies like Social Security, unemployment insurance, and a federal minimum wage. Perkins had a long history of advocating for workers' rights, with a particular focus on protections for women, children, and immigrants. In 1911, Perkins, then head of the New York office of the National Consumers League, witnessed the devastating Triangle Factory fire which caused the deaths of 146 female workers. Former President Teddy Roosevelt recommended her to lead the city's Committee on Safety, where she fought for legislation that mandated fire drills, marked fire exits, sprinkler systems, and occupancy limits. Before FDR tapped Perkins for his cabinet, she held several roles in New York state government, implementing workplace and fire safety reforms that were adopted throughout the country.

He organized community programs like free breakfasts and worked to unite people across racial groups to fight poverty and injustice.

On Saturday February 14th, Cedar Cliff's  Archery Team competed at The Camp Hill Lion's State Qualifying Tournament. The Colts team of 15 boys and girls, Captained by Natasha Noss, took home 3rd place, with 11 schools represented at the event. The team's leading scorers were Jaliyah Borrero, Feron Donelan, and Carson Griswold. The team will seek to enter another state qualifying tournament on February 28th prior to the state finals on March 20th held at Spooky Nook Sports Arena. Cedar Cliff's Athletic Dept and Outdoor Adventure class is expecting a new shipment of archery equipment in the coming weeks. If you are interested in participating in the archery club please provide your contact information to Lieutenant Colonel Johnson, Mr. Weaver, Mr. Weedon, Mr. Simpson, or Ms. Ede for future information once it's available.

Did you hear? It’s official. The awesome Unified Bocce Team has earned its way to the Commonwealth Division Bocce Playoffs for the 1st time ever!  It is also the 1st time both Cedar Cliff and Redland will be battling to go to represent at Regionals. Congrats to both West Shore School District teams!  The playoff game will be held at East Penn High School this Thursday, starting at 4 pm. Let’s fill the stands! Go Colts!

Have nothing to do after school this Wednesday? Come out to watch some of your favorite Cedar Cliff staff attempt to win against our awesome Unified Bocce Team starting at 3:15 pm. in gym 2.  Totally free event!  It’s the 2nd annual “Staff vs. Bocce Team” Event. Come watch to find out.

Did you know that producing AI hardware requires mining rare earth minerals like Neodymium and Dysprosium? And now a special Black History Month announcement, as we celebrate the achievements, history, and impact of Black leaders throughout the month. At just 15 years old, Claudette Colvin refused to give up her bus seat in 1955, months before Rosa Parks. Her bravery helped challenge bus segregation laws and played an important role in the civil rights movement.

Did you know that training a single major AI model like OpenAI or ChatGPT-4 can emit carbon dioxide levels equal to the lifetime emissions of multiple cars? Furthermore, by 2030, AI growth could add 24 to 44 million metric tons of CO2 annually. Join the Environmental Club to help lessen the negative impacts of widespread AI usage now! And now a special Black History Month announcement, as we celebrate the achievements, history, and impact of Black leaders throughout the month. Harriet Tubman escaped slavery and returned many times to guide others to freedom through the Underground Railroad. She later supported the Union during the Civil War and fought for women’s rights. She is remembered for her courage and leadership.

The Unified Bocce Teams battled the panthers of East Penn yesterday with 5 W;s in the win column and almost won the 6th game by two points. And now a special Black History Month announcement, as we celebrate the achievements, history, and impact of Black leaders throughout the month. Frederick Douglass was an abolitionist, writer, and powerful speaker who escaped slavery. His autobiographies exposed the realities of slavery and helped grow the movement to end it. He also supported women’s rights and equal citizenship.

Congratulations to our Cedar Cliff student authors! Last week, the Alliance for Young Artists & Writers, in partnership with Shippensburg University, presented students with the 2026 Scholastic Art & Writing Awards. Seven talented student authors from Cedar Cliff have won awards. Congratulations to our Honorable Mention winners: Jenna Sadowski, 12th grade, for Poetry; Jadyn Arroyo, 12th grade, for Flash Fiction; Dzejla Kunovac, 12th grade, for Senior Portfolio; Amelia Morgan, 9th grade, for Novel; and Carmella Santorello, 9th grade, for Speculative Fiction. Congratulations also to our Silver Key Award winners: Jenna Sadowski, 12th grade, for Short Story; Jadyn Arroyo, 12th grade, for Critical Essay; and Scarlett Gendron, 11th grade, for Poetry. We also recognize our Gold Key Award winners — the highest honor in Scholastic Writing — Jadyn Arroyo, 12th grade, for Poetry; Dzejla Kunovac, 12th grade, for Flash Fiction; and Eduardo Lara Ortiz, 11th grade, for Poetry. Student writing was reviewed and recognized by a panel of creative professionals and awarded for its outstanding merit in originality, skill, and the emergence of a personal voice and vision. It was a highly competitive field this year with less than 10% of student entries winning silver keys awards and only 5% of student entries winning the highest honor of gold key awards. Good luck to our gold key winners: Eduardo Lara Ortiz, Jadyn Arroyo, and Dzjela Kunovac. Their work now advances to the National level for judging! Since 1923, the Awards have recognized some of the nation’s most celebrated artists and writers while they were teenagers, including Stephen King, Amanda Gorman, Charles White, and Andy Warhol. We are proud to count our Cedar Cliff students among these esteemed Scholastic Art & Writing Awards alumni!

Malcolm X was a powerful civil rights leader and speaker who promoted Black pride, self-respect, and self-determination. On Tuesday afternoon the Freshman Boys basketball season wrapped up their season by defeating Milton Hershey 46-30 to finish the season 11-4!  The Boys defeated every League team at least once this season and gave some payback to Milton Hershey yesterday for a loss in early Jan.   The Colts were led yesterday by Gavin Bryan with 10 pts, his season high.  Harrison Brown contributed 9 pts. Sam Kocher chipped in with his season high of 8 pts.  Cameron Regis added 6 pts while Maurice Burgos scored 5 pts. Ethan Ebbert had 5 pts and Gavin Deveney hit a trey for his 3 pts. Owen Lyons sank 1 of 2 FTs for his point.  Congratulations Boys on a very successful season!  We are PROUD of you for an amazing and highly entertaining season!

Last night, the Cedar Cliff Colts swim teams competed against Red Land. The girls earned a strong victory with a final score of 99–84. First-place finishes for Cedar Cliff included Kayleigh Marshall in the 200 Freestyle, Tenley Turney in the 200 IM, Sam Shoop in the 100 Butterfly, and the 400 Freestyle Relay team of Libby McCurdy, Lucy Winger, Tenley Turney, and Sam Shoop. On the boys’ side, Cedar Cliff battled hard but fell to Red Land with a final score of 97–70. First-place finishes for the Colts included Declan Ryan in the 200 Freestyle and 100 Freestyle, Samuel Bitting in the 200 IM and 100 Backstroke, Owyn Schweitzer in the 500 Freestyle, and the 400 Freestyle Relay team of Nate Gannon, Samuel Bitting, Hunter Hockenbraugh, and Declan Ryan. Great job to all of our swimmers—keep up the hard work, Colts!

Do I need to provide my height and weight in order to receive my cap and gown? YES!
Do girls have to wear a white dress under their gown? No!
Do I need tickets for graduation? No, the Farm Show Complex has a lot of seating.
Can I decorate my cap? Yes, but nothing may hang over the edges, and they must be appropriate.
How do I order transcripts? Current students must request or view transcripts on SchooLinks.  Once a student graduates, they must order using the Cedar Cliff Guidance page.

Cedar Cliff Honors-AP-College Summer Work

At Cedar Cliff High School, our athletics program is built on the belief that true success begins in the classroom and extends onto the field of play. We are committed to developing student-athletes who demonstrate excellence, discipline, and integrity in all aspects of their lives. By fostering a culture that prioritizes academic achievement alongside athletic performance, we ensure our students are prepared not only for competition, but for future opportunities beyond high school. Through teamwork, accountability, and strong support systems, Cedar Cliff Athletics empowers every student to reach their full potential—both as scholars and as competitors. We expect our student-athletes to hold themselves to high standards of sportsmanship, and hope parents and fans will serve as role models for our students by exhibiting high standards of sportsmanship as well.
Cedar Cliff Athletics (@cedarcliff_ad) • Instagram photos and videos

Additional College Options - Dual Enrollment
Social Studies/History AP - Honors - College
Cedar Cliff High School offers numerous higher-level courses.  Some of those courses are designated as Honors or Advanced Placement and may require summer work prior to the course.

Many College in High School (CHS) courses are offered. Students earn both high school and college credits. CHS courses do not require summer work, but students must enroll in the course at the college for credit.  Additionally, a textbook rental or purchase may be required. Some college courses have eligibility requirements.  Students pay reduced CHS tuition for college credits, except for the free Business courses.
Click on the department for details regarding each course.

Welcome to the Cedar Cliff High School National Honor Society!
NHS Membership Criteria for Consideration

The National Honor Society honors student leaders who have excellent scholarship, leadership, service, and character and helps them to grow into a life of service, learning, and success.

Juniors and Seniors (who are not already members) who have achieved a GPA of 95% will be sent applications late in the summer. All members are carefully selected, based on the National Honor Society tenets of scholarship, leadership, service, and character. A formal induction ceremony is held each November, although new members are accepted and must begin completion of the service requirement shortly after their admission in September.

Community service is a transformative learning experience that promotes personal growth and builds character.  That is why the West Shore School District requires that all of our National Honor Society members must complete 30 hours of community service during each year of membership.  Service hours for Seniors must be completed by the beginning of May.  Seniors may also submit hours from service provided during the Summer before their Senior year.  Service hours for Juniors must be completed by the beginning of June.  Failure to submit these hours will result in dismissal from the organization.  Please see the NHS bulletin board outside Room 116 for Service Opportunities and other information.  Please remember to submit your hours, so they can be counted!

Students can access the hours tracking sheet at all times on Google. Students who have not completed half of their service hours at that time are placed on probation and urged to see Mrs. Higgins promptly to rectify the situation.

Attendance at NHS Meetings is mandatory, except under extenuating circumstances.  If you are going to miss a meeting, you must contact either Mrs. Higgins or the officers at least 24 hours before the meeting. If you miss a meeting for an unexpected reason, please contact Mrs. Higgins ASAP.
NHS Meetings are held on the third Thursday of each month at 7:05 am in the Auditorium.  Be there early to sign in. Attendance is MANDATORY.

Committed members who participate in Cedar Cliff National Honor Society activities can earn all their service hours through our activities and many members complete their service hours by midyear.  However, this takes serious commitment.  Members who fall behind on service hours may have to seek out service activities to meet the thirty hour annual requirement.  Those students must contact local organizations to find volunteer opportunities.

These organizations frequently have volunteer opportunities available: senior centers, pet shelters, churches, political parties, local parks and recreation, food banks, hospitals, fire departments, community sports organizations, libraries, thrift stores (such as Community Aid or Salvation Army), homeless shelters, and community parks (outdoor cleanup).  Students may also engage in charitable fundraising, assisting the elderly, and helping at school events.  Hours must be reported with contact information for all these activities.
Activities Not Counted as National Honor Society Service

Any activities that you receive payment or credit for do not count as community service for the National Honor Society.  Similarly, activities that do not specifically serve the community, such as playing in a band, singing in a choir, or attending youth groups do not count as service hours.  An exception to this rule is when those activities do provide direct service to the community.  For example, if your band plays at a charitable event or if your youth group assists the elderly, that is legitimate community service.  Additionally, family activities do not count as community service.  These include housework, yard work, caring for siblings, assisting parents at home, or other activities that do not serve the broader community. Finally, common favors to competent friends and neighbors that do not serve the broader community are also not considered community service. These include helping friends with chores, providing services to private (for profit) businesses, pet care, and private babysitting.  One exception that can be counted as public service is offering tutoring to other students, which requires approval by any teacher.

Interscholastic Athletic Disclosure Forms

Activity fees assist the district with its efforts to continue to provide a robust offering of athletics, fine arts, and other student activities. This fee is charged to students in grade seven through twelve (7-12) participating on any district athletic team or color guard/indoor guard and marching band. Accommodations will be made for students/families experiencing economic hardships as well as for those students who qualify for the Free/Reduced student meal program. A list of activities for which fees will be charged can be found on the Activity Fee chart available as a download below.

It is important to note that this is not a participation fee that guarantees any playing time or level of participation; it is a payment required of all students rostered in a sport or activity. As the rosters have been finalized and submitted to the administration by coaches and directors, refunds will be made upon request for those students who are cut or not selected for a team. Students who quit activities after rosters/lists are finalized will still be subject to payment of the fee.
The fee structure includes an individual cap as well as a family cap as follows:
Individual cap: $190 - If a student participates in several activities, no more than $190 in total will be due per individual student for all activities within the current school year.
Family cap: $380 - For families with more than one child participating in several activities, no more than $380 in total will be due per family for activities within the current school year.
Families who would like to pay Athletic/Student Activity Fees online. Please click here.      Notice:  New Payment Method for Athletic/Student Activity Fees - Effective June 2, 2025.
First National Bank (FNB) does not store your card or banking information
An electronic receipt will be emailed to you upon completion of your payment

Families who prefer not to utilize the electronic system, should submit a check made payable to West Shore School District. Checks should be submitted to the high school where the student participates in the activity or sport.  Middle school students should submit payment to the high school where they participate in the activity.

Take I-81 South to Exit 12, Plainfield. Bear right off exit, onto Rt. 465, to the stop sign. Go straight across Rt. #11. At next stop sign turn left onto Rt. 641; go approximately seven miles to Newville. High school sits on left side approaching Newville.

Take 81 South to Exit 12, Plainfield. Bear right off exit to the stop sign. Turn leftonto Route 11. Travel 5.5 miles turning right onto Mt. Rock Road (right by a United Methodist Church). School is located 3 miles straight ahead.
1 Crusader Way, HarrisburgBasketball: High School GymSoccer: Rocco Ortenzio Stadium, 1 Crusader WayBaseball: Penbrook Field, 3106A Hoffer Street, Penbrook

Take Trindle road through Mechanicsburg. After passing the IGA make a left onto rt. 174 (Boiling Springs Road). Stay on route 174 approx. 7 miles to the square at Boiling Springs. Make a right at the clock tower; school is approx. 2 blocks on left.

From Harrisburg: Route 422 East (approximately 18 miles to Lebanon), passing through Hershey, Palmyra, Annville, Cleona to 12th Street in Lebanon. Turn right onto 12th Street. At the second traffic light turn left onto Walnut Street and proceed one block to the intersection of Tenth & Walnut. Turn right onto Tenth Street and proceed staying in the lane that becomes the Cornwall Road (do not bear right!). Proceed two miles to the next traffic light and turn left onto Evergreen Road. Cedar Crest High School is 1/2 mile located on the left side of the highway.
From the PA Turnpike: Route 72 north to Quentin to Rocherty to Evergreen Rd. to the school (see directions above from Lancaster).

Take I-81 South to exit 45, Turn right at light. Which is College Street. Bear right and to a very short distance and make a left on Belvedere Street (there's a medical center). Go a couple blocks to High Street where you make a left on High Street and go to the red light. Make a right turn at the light and the road comes to a "Y" bare to the right, which is Orange St. This road comes to a dead end and you will see the High School.

From the north or east: Follow I- 81 South to the Manada Hill exit 77. Turn right off the exit onto Rt 39 West, proceed 2 miles to the traffic light at Piketown Rd. Turn left onto Piketown Rd. and immediately left into the school.

From the south or west: Follow I-81 North to the Linglestown Exit 72B. Turn right off the exit onto Mountain Rd. Proceed to the flag pole in Linglestown square. Turn right onto Rt 39 East; continue for 1.5 miles to the traffic light at Piketown Rd. Turn right onto Piketown Rd. and immediately left into the school.
North Rt. 83 and get off at the Union Deposit Exit. At the red light make a left onto Union Deposit Road. Go to the second light which is Rutherford Road and the school sits on the left side.

From Interstate 83 South take Exit 24 - Emigsville. Turn left at the light at the end of the ramp. Go to the 2nd light and turn right onto N. George Street. Go to the next light and turn left onto Emig Rd. Emig Rd. turns into Mundis Mill Rd. Go 1.6 miles and the school is on the left.

Use exit 6 off I-81 South. Turn right off the exit ramp and turn left at the first traffic light. Turn right at the first stop sign (McKinley Street) and proceed straight for quite a number of blocks. The High School parking lot is on the right.

Use Exit 16 off I-81 South. Turn right and go to the 4th traffic light (Fifth Avenue). Turn right, after which the road winds around T.D. Woods Manufacturing; go under an overpass, come to a light, turn right onto Scotland Avenue and go three (3) blocks and the school is on the left.

Take 581 to I-81 South. Get off I-81 South at Mechanicsburg exit (Rt. 114). At the stop sign, make a left onto Rt. 114 and go to the Sheetz gas station and make a right onto Rt. 11. Go through New Kingstown and school will be on the right.

Take Rt. 11-15 North going past KFC, you make a left onto Shady Lane. Continue going straight to stop sign (which is Enola Drive). Cross over and keep going straight and you will see the school on your right.

To get to the middle school, follow same directions, except when you get to Enola Drive make a right turn, go past football field which is on your left, go up the hill and watch for sign for middle school, which will be on your left.

On Rt. 15 South, take the exit bypass at Hunterstown exit/Business 15 South/Gettysburg College. Follow the signs to Gettysburg on Rt. 15 South. When approaching town, the Ames Shopping Center will be on your left. Next to the shopping center on the left is the Eisenhower Elementary School (hockey is played here).

To go to the high school, continue past the elementary school to the first red light. Turn left on Business 15 continuing straight through the square. Go through two traffic lights and at the bottom of a hill you will see the Junior High School on your left (Lefever Street) - turn left onto campus. The football/soccer/cross country fields are behind the Junior High School.
The Senior High School is further back on the left - site of the gymnasium and tennis courts.

Take the turnpike east toward Philadelphia, get off at the Reading Exit (# 21).Take Route 222 North 7 miles into Shillington(this will be a four lane highway turning into a two lane highway). Once in Shillington, there will be a light at a pretzel factory. Counting that light, go nine lights before turning right (staying on 222 North the whole time). After turning right, the school is one block away straight ahead.
Take I-83 North to the Hershey exit and follow Rt. 322 East. As you pass

Lower Dauphin High School on your left, go about a mile and Rt. 322 East will bear to the right past the Hershey Medical Center. Follow that road for several mile, passing through two red lights (first light Don's Pier is on the right - second light is Cocoa Ave).Go straight through for about one-half mile and make a left turn onto Homestead Road. High School will be on the left.

Take I-83 South to exit # 1 (Shrewsbury). At the stop sign at the end of the ramp turn left onto Rt. 851. Staying on Rt. 851 East, go through the towns of Stewartstown and New Park. Bear right at the " Y " in New Park heading for Fawn Grove. The High School is located on Rt. 851 about 3 miles after taking the right at the Y.

Take I-83 North to the Hershey exit and follow Rt. 322 East. Go approximately six to seven miles until you come to the Hummelstown exit. At stop sign make a left, cross the bridge and the school is on the right.

Take Rt. 283 East to the Manheim-Mt. Joy exit. At the stop sign turn left and go about five (5) miles to Manheim (you are now on High Street when you get into town). Go through both red lights and turn left when you get to Laurel Street. Go two (2) blocks, turn right and you will see the school.
Manheim Township High School Gene Kruis Field at War Memorial Stadium115 Blue Streak Blvd, Lancaster
Get on Trindle Road. Stay to the right onto Simpson Street. Go 2 Blocks make a Right onto Broad Street, Senior High is 2 blocks on right.

Memorial Park Take Main Street to Washington Street, make a right and park is there Northside Elementary - Take Simpson Ferry Road make a left onto Walnut Street Follow until left onto Schoolside Drive field on left.

Take Rt. 283 East toward Lancaster. Take the exit for the Harrisburg International Airport and then take Rt. 441 exit. When you come to the stop sign make a left and follow Rt. 441 for several miles and the school will be on the right. (This road is a very windy two-lane road).

Take Rt. 322 to Hershey going past the Hershey Medical Center on your right and the Hershey Motor Lodge on your left. Continue on Rt. 322 for a couple of miles until reaching Homestead Road (the New Hershey High School is on the left). Turn right onto Homestead Road to the corner on Bachmanville Road , turn left on Bachmanville Road and go to the 1st left, turn left and the soccer/field hockey fields will be on the right. For football, continue past the soccer/hockey fields turning right onto Spartan Lane with field straight ahead on the left (Stadium Parking is located behind their Middle School).

Take Rt. 83 S to Emigsville ( # 11 ) exit. Cross over Rt. 83 turning left at first road (Board Road). Travel on Board Rd for a couple of miles until first stop sign: turn right. At next stop sign, turn left (Chevy dealership on lef ). At traffic light, turn right. At First Street, turn left onto High Street. The school is three blocks ahead on right.

Take Rt. 15 South to Dillsburg. Go to the second light on Rt. 15 (first light - PNC Bank is on your left; second light is where you want to make a left turn). Go into square of town and at red light make a right turn and follow that road for a mile or two. Jr. High School will be on your right; Sr. High School on your left.

Football Field: Take Rt. 422 into Palmyra. At the square, which is Railroad Street, turn right. Go one block and turn right again onto Cherry Street. Go one block again and turn left onto Locust Street. Football field is on your left.
High School: Take Rt. 422 into Palmyra. At the square, which is Railroad Street, turn right. Go to " Y " in road and bear left. School is several hundred yards on right.

Take I-81 South to Shippensburg and get off at Exit #29. At the stop sign bear right and travel approximately 2 to 3 miles (passing the strip mall on your left) until you come to the traffic light (CVS will be in front of you). Make a left turn; go through town and look for the Greyhound Diner on your left. Turn right to go to Veterans Memorial Stadium. Parking is around and on the right. If you’re going to the school, follow the road to the left and then turn right at the stop sign. The high school will be on the right.

Take Rt. 283 South to Rohrerstown Road exit. Go straight on Rohrerstown Road past New Danville Pike. About 1/4 mile past New Danville Pike (Turkey Hill Market) turn left at the Cherry Hill Orchards, go straight into Quarreyville. As you enter Quarreyville, go straight unto you get to the second red light (St. Paul's Lutheran Church). Turn right, and proceed about two miles. Solanco H.S. is on the right.

Take Rt. 15 South to the York Springs exit. At stop sign make a left onto Rt. 94 South and go to the center crossroads of Hanover; this road is Carlisle Street into Hanover, and at the square the street changes to Baltimore Street. Proceed two (2) miles and you'll see the South Hanover shopping center and a Sunoco gas station. Bear left onto Black Rock Road, go about one-quarter mile and make a left onto Bowman Road. The school will be right in front of you.
Take 83 North, Go across the South Bridge take the 2nd Street Exit. Make a right at light onto Paxton Street. Make a right at next light onto Cameron Street to Steelton turn left onto Swatara Street.
Take I-81 to I-78 exiting at Route 61 South. Continue on 61S through Leesport to traffic light. Turn left at light onto Route 73 East. School is on the left.

Take 83 North to 81 South and get off at the Progress Street exit and bear right until you come to the red light which is Elmerton Avenue; make a left and the school will be on your left. Roscoe Werner Stadium is located directly behind the high school.
Susquenita is located between Marysville and Duncannon off routes 11 & 15. Take 11/15 North School is on left approx. 6 miles from Marysville
Take Rt. 283 to Rt. 30 toward Lancaster. Get off at the Lititz exit (501 NORTH). At the 5th red light after the airport turn left onto Orange Street. The school is on Orange St. about 3 blocks ahead.

Take 81 South to the first Greencastle exit (Exit 3). At the bottom of the ramp, turn left. Go 8 miles straight into Waynesboro. Continue through town to a McDonalds. Turn right at the first stop light past McDonalds. At next stop sign, turn left and take the first entrance into the school.

Take I-83 South to York and get off at Exit 10. At the end of the ramp there is a stop sign. Continue straight through until you get to the red light, which is Rt. 30 West. Make a right and go several miles until you get to the Carlisle Road exit. At the light make a left turn onto Rt. 74. Go about a mile until you get to McDonald's; turn right, go two blocks to Banniter Street and there you will see the school.

Interscholastic Athletic Disclosure Forms

The West Shore Natatorium is located adjacent to Red Land High School, 560 Fishing Creek Road. Lewisberry, PA. The facility was built in 1987. It has 6 lanes for 25 yards and this can be changed to a 6 lane 25 meter course. A spectator area is above the pool deck. The natatorium is located off of Exit 36 on I83. I83 south from Camp Hill or Harrisburg you turn right onto Fishing Creek Road. When coming from York, I83 North, you turn left off the Exit 36. Red Land High School is located 1/2 mile on the left. Follow the driveway and bear left to front of the school. The natatorium is the last building on the right at the south end of the complex.

The West Shore School District requires aquatics in 2nd, 4th, and 6th grades, and also offers two High School electives. In second and fourth grade, swimming instruction focuses on water orientation, improved stroke technique, and safety.  The Pennsylvania Fish and Boat Commission Boating Safety Awareness Course is the requirement in sixth grade. In High School students have the option of taking two swim electives.  First, we have the Swim and Skill class which is an introduction to a number of water-related activities and skills such as: swim technique, swim meets, basic lifeguarding skills, splash ball, basic water rescue, snorkeling, spring board diving, water polo, canoeing, and fitness.  The second elective offered is the Swim Endurance class which offers a fitness based swim class and introduction to life long aquatic based activities such as:  distance swimming, sprinting, aquatic races, shallow and deep water aerobics, swim and gym, and water running.  Juniors and Seniors receive Physical Education credit for these two swim elective classes.

The West Shore Natatorium is the home facility to both Cedar Cliff and Red Land High School swimming and diving teams.  Swimmers and divers from both teams have competed successfully at the Mid-Penn, District III, and PIAA championships levels.  For more information please see link below.
The facility is shared with the West Shore Recreation Commission who offer a variety of aquatic programs throughout the year.

Pennsylvania State Police Megan's Law Website

As a school district, the safety and welfare of our students, staff, and visitors to our schools and facilities is our highest priority. In order to maintain a safe environment for all members of the West Shore community, we have decided to take additional security measures at the West Shore Stadium.

In addition to visual inspections, all guests to the West Shore Stadium will be subject to a bag check before entering the facility for football games. Following this new procedure, the District will restrict the size and type of bags permitted into the West Shore Stadium.
To expedite entry into the stadium, visitors are strongly encouraged not to bring any bags with them; however, one of the following will be permitted for each adult/child with a ticket to the event:
One clear bag per person. No larger than 12" x 6" x 12". A one-gallon clear freezer bag (Ziploc or similar) is acceptable as meeting this requirement.
One small clutch bag or purse no larger than 6.5" x 4.5", with or without a strap.
Diaper bags, strollers, walkers, and other medically necessary items will be permitted following a detailed search.
Clothing, blankets, cameras, and cell phones will be permitted if carried loosely or in an approved clear bag.
Seat cushions will be permitted but must not exceed 18 inches wide and cannot contain pockets, zippers or concealable areas.
There are no provisions to leave or check items at the gates. Fans will be asked to take prohibited items back to their vehicle.
Stadium gates open 90 minutes prior to the start of each home game. Please give yourself time to park and proceed through the bag check.

We feel limiting the size and types of bags allowed into the West Shore Stadium is an important additional layer of security for our District athletes, musicians, and spectators. Thank you for your assistance and compliance with these safety procedures.

Interscholastic Athletic Disclosure Forms

All primary, intermediate and middle school students must be accompanied and supervised by an adult at all times. Unaccompanied elementary and middle school students will not be permitted to enter the stadium.

Unaccompanied high school students will be asked to present their student ID. We realize they may not have them for the first home game, but as they are issued, the expectation will be that students show them for entry to the game.
Students will not be permitted to gather in groups at the ticket gates and concession areas. The expectation is that all students watch the game from the stands.
Fencing has been placed in the grassy areas of the West Shore Stadium to restrict access to these areas.

Students must also follow the West Shore Stadium Bag Policy (click here to review). Students should not attempt to bring Chromebooks or other unnecessary items (skateboards, scooters, footballs, large backpacks, etc.) to the stadium.
Generally speaking re-entry is not permitted to the stadium.
Athletic Health and Safety Plan for resuming sports during COVID-19

Fall Season - Football, Boys/Girls Soccer, Field Hockey, Golf, Girls Tennis, Boys/Girls Cross Country, Girls Volleyball, and Cheerleading. Cross Country, Field Hockey and Football are also available to seventh and eighth grade middle school boys and girls.

Winter Season - Boys/Girls Basketball, Boys/Girls Wrestling, Boys/Girls Swimming and Diving, Cheerleading and Coed Unified Bocce. Boys/Girls Basketball and Boys/Girls Wrestling are also available to seventh and eighth grade middle school boys and girls.

Spring Season - Baseball, Softball, Boys/Girls Lacrosse, Boys/Girls Track and Field, Boys Tennis, and Boys Volleyball. Boys/Girls Soccer, Boys/Girls Track, Girls Volleyball are also available to seventh and eighth grade middle school boys and girls.

Community Bulletin Board & Guidelines for Distribution of Fliers at Schools
Information about Free or Reduced Price Meals
Your household must meet eligibility requirements for your child(ren) to receive free or reduced price lunches at school.

You may submit an application with your household income information for your child(ren) to qualify for free or reduced price lunches. Applications are available at www.compass.dhs.pa.gov and https.//linqconnect.com/main.

If any member of your household receives Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) benefits, Temporary Assistance for Needy Families (TANF) benefits, your child automatically qualifies for free lunches. You should receive a notification from West Shore School District, informing you that you do not need to submit an application. If you or a household member receives SNAP or TANF benefits, but you do not receive a notification, you may submit an application with your case number to qualify for free lunches.
You may also submit an application to qualify for free lunches based on your child’s status as a homeless, runaway, migrant, or foster child. You may indicate your child’s status on the application.
A first breakfast will still be available at no charge. Lunch will cost $3.40 for primary/intermediate schools and $3.50/$3.85 for middle/high schools.

Community Bulletin Board & Guidelines for Distribution of Fliers at Schools

Student Meal Accounts
                    
                            Student Accounts

Parents/Guardians are responsible for all charges on their student’s account and are encouraged to frequently check the account balance to ensure adequate funds are available for their student(s) to purchase school breakfasts, lunches, and a la carte items.

If a student owes more than $10.00 on their account, a cafeteria staff member will attempt to make a courtesy call (or send a letter) to inform parents/guardians of the student’s negative meal account balance and request an immediate deposit to the account. If the negative balance exceeds $30.00, the building principal will contact the parents/guardians to discuss circumstances.

Collection of Outstanding BalancesIf a student has a negative meal account balance at the close of the school year or following a transfer to another district, the meal account balance will be converted to a parent/guardian financial obligation.  The financial obligation will remain the responsibility of the student’s parents/guardians until it is paid in full in compliance with Board Policy 808.

Requesting an Account Refund or Balance Transfer (revised 7.8.25)Positive balances for underclassmen will be automatically carried over to the next school year. Refunds from student meal accounts are granted when a student graduates, leaves the District, or a special circumstance necessitates the refund.

Upon withdrawing from the District, students must bring their account to a zero balance. Graduating seniors may request a refund or may request that balances be transferred to another student enrolled in the District. Positive balances for underclassmen will be carried over to the following year.
Students receiving free and reduced-priced meals shall be treated under these same guidelines.

Parents/Guardians are responsible for all charges on their student’s account and are encouraged to frequently check the account balance to ensure adequate funds are available for their student(s) to purchase school breakfasts, lunches, and a la carte items.

Course Sequencing & Credit Recommendations
Credit Requirements & Grade Level Promotion
Entering Course Selections in PowerSchool
Only one core English course may be scheduled per school year. For example, a student may not complete both Grade 9 and Grade 10 English during the freshman year.

U.S. History Part II is a required Social Studies course for students in Grade 9. Other courses with specific grade-level designations must be scheduled in the appropriate year throughout a student’s high school career.
Any exceptions to required course sequencing must be approved by the administration.
The charts below shows the recommended number of credits a student should take each year in order to graduate from the West Shore School District in four years. For a larger version, click here.
Student Credit & Placement Considerations

Students who do not successfully complete a high school course during the school year may have the opportunity to enroll in summer school for credit recovery, when feasible and when sufficient enrollment exists. Students are strongly encouraged to successfully complete all courses during the regular school year.
Please note that additional costs are associated with summer school, and the District cannot guarantee that all failed courses will be offered each summer.

Credit Requirements & Grade Level Promotion
Entering Course Selections in PowerSchool
MINIMUM CREDIT TOTAL Class of 2027 & 2028
MINIMUM CREDIT TOTAL Class of 2027 & 2028
RECOMMENDED MINIMUM CREDITS Class of 2029
RECOMMENDED MINIMUM CREDITS Class of 2030

Students in grades 9-11 are required to sign up for seven (7) credits per year, unless choosing a study hall in lieu of a course.  To be promoted from one grade level to the next, students must earn the following credits:
Ten (10) credits to move from sophomore to junior status
Sixteen (16) credits to move from junior to senior status (credits must be earned prior to the senior year)
Five (5) credits to move from freshman to sophomore status
Eleven (11) credits to move from sophomore to junior status
Seventeen (17) credits to move from junior to senior status (credits must be earned prior to the senior year)
Six (6) credits to move from freshman to sophomore status
Twelve (12) credits to move from sophomore to junior status
Eighteen (18) credits to move from junior to senior status (credits must be earned prior to the senior year)

Course Selection & Scheduling Information
Course Sequencing & Credit Recommendations
Credit Requirements & Grade Level Promotion
Entering Course Selections in PowerSchool

Academic Contracts (Board Policy 118) are offered to properly qualified students.  Contracts will be planned at the building level, follow the requirements for a planned course, include minimal time and report requirements, assessment standards, (written and/or oral), and include regular supervisory conferences between the student and a teacher.
The student seeking approval for an academic contract project shall be required to:
Request an application from the counselor.
Complete the application with the participating teacher.
Submit the application to the building principal for approval.
Submit the application for approval to the Director of Secondary Education.
The following guidelines relative to academic contracting are suggested:
Contracting to make up a failed course will only be done in summer school if the course is not available through regular summer school offerings and regular school year scheduling.
No summer contracting will be offered in co-op or Pathway Internship.
Summer school contracts will be available only for remedial purposes.

Remedial contracting will only be available for seniors (at least 16 credits after summer school the third year) during the school year. Seniors may only contract for one remedial credit during the school year. A maximum of 7 credits should be earned during a regular school year.
Online courses for remedial purposes may be permitted during the school year.
Suggested guidelines for academic year credits are:
The maximum number of credits (regular, classroom, and/or contracting) that can be earned during summer school is 2.
The maximum number of credits which can be earned by a student between the first day of school and the close of summer school each year is 9 unless prior administrative approval is granted.

Contracting for underclassmen will be considered as an option for acceleration purposes when the regular schedule cannot accommodate the student or when the course/class requested is beyond the scope and sequence offered at the high school.

Students wishing to access this option must request consideration in writing to the principal of the building by June 1st of the year preceding the planned program. This can be done through the completion of an Academic Enrichment Contract.
The student must provide a brochure or pamphlet outlining the program and explaining the course of study when the application is made.
A committee from the school will determine approval or disapproval of all requests.

It is the student’s responsibility to ensure that grades from the program are reported to the school quarterly in time for inclusion on the report card, if deemed appropriate. Otherwise no credit will be given.
Final grades for this course must be provided to the school no later than the end of the semester.
Failure to meet the above requirements may result in no further requests for future exceptions being considered.
No student may take more than 2 credits per year in this manner and must take a minimum of 4 credits per year at the high school.

Course Selection & Scheduling Information
Course Sequencing & Credit Recommendations
Credit Requirements & Grade Level Promotion
Entering Course Selections in PowerSchool
Has satisfactorily completed 17 credits in previous high school years.
Has an earned average of 86% in high school courses completed to date.
Applied to and been accepted by a college.

If attending college on a part-time basis enrolls in a minimum of two credits per year at the home school, including courses required for graduation, or if attending college as a full-time student, elects courses that will meet the high school graduation requirements.
Agrees that student's family accepts responsibility for all costs for matriculation at the college.
Submits a copy of his/her college grades to the high school at the established intervals observed by the college.
Provides transportation to the college at no cost to the District.
Maintains a satisfactory standing as determined by the college and high school in the program.
Accepts the possibility of withdrawal from the college courses and the ensuing difficulty of returning to full-time classes at the high school.
Informs the high school of his/her intent by January 31 to participate in the graduation ceremony.
Realizes that the student will no longer be ranked with his/her graduating class.
A senior may attend on a part-time basis an approved and duly licensed/accredited trade, technical, or vocational school if he/she meets the following requirements:
Has satisfactorily completed 17 credits in previous high school years.
Is performing satisfactorily as determined by a high school committee composed of one counselor, two faculty members (including one cooperative education teacher), and a building administrator.
Has applied and has been accepted at an approved trade, technical or vocational school.
Enrolls in a minimum of two credits at his/her high school, including credits in any required courses for graduation.
Accepts responsibility for all costs of matriculation at such approved school.
Provides transportation to and from approved school.
Maintains a satisfactory standing at both schools as determined by the committee and approved school.
Submits copies of trade school marks to the high school at the intervals used by the trade school.
Recognizes the possibility of withdrawal from the trade school and the ensuing difficulty of returning to full-time classes at the high school.
Informs the high school of his/her intent by March 1 to participate in the graduation ceremony.

Course Selection & Scheduling Information
Course Sequencing & Credit Recommendations
Credit Requirements & Grade Level Promotion
Entering Course Selections in PowerSchool
Undertake a program of studies equivalent to a minimum of six credits.
Enroll in courses abroad to parallel the required courses established by the District for graduation (If English is not available, the language of the country may be substituted.).
Provide a transcript of grades from the foreign school for review by the home school staff. Determination of credits satisfactorily completed cannot be made until the transcript is received.
Have satisfactorily completed the competency requirements of the District.
Bear all costs of travel and education while abroad.
Notify the District by January 31 of intent to participate in graduation.
Recognize that in the event of returning to the home school during the year, difficulties may exist in reentering a full-time program of study.
Realize that course work abroad will not be calculated for GPA and class rank.

Junior Reserve Officers’ Training Corps (JROTC)

While art courses may generally be elected by any student in any curriculum, the art curriculum does prepare the individual for a professional career in art. It offers a broad art education that provides a basis for entrance into a college or art school. It also provides experiences necessary for employment in art or art-related fields.

DRAWING & PAINTING I202501 .5 Credit      1.0 WeightThis
is the introductory course to the Drawing and Painting Visual Arts
Pathway. The activities are intended to
provide exposure to a number of studio concepts and experiences pursued in the
visual arts program. Observational skill
building, spatial analysis and composition development are emphasized using a
variety of media and 2-D processes within the framework of aesthetic,
historical, and critical understanding.
DRAWING & PAINTING II202502.5 Credit      1.0 WeightPrerequisite(s): Satisfactory Completion of Drawing & Painting

This course continues to explore the Drawing & Painting Visual Arts Pathway through drawing, painting, and printmaking. A range of subject matter such as still life, portraiture, landscape, and nature are investigated through a wide variety of techniques and 2D media. Observational drawing and painting continues to be an emphasis in this course. The next step for a student wishing to continue on this Visual Arts Pathways is to enroll in the Independent Studio, L3 course.

ANIMATION I202727.5 Credit      1.0 WeightThis is the introductory course to the Animation Visual Arts Pathway. The course is designed as an introduction to working creatively with moving images as a communication tool. Students complete several short and long-term projects that utilize both 3-D and 2-D traditional/digital animation techniques. Projects are thematic in nature and deal with personal, social, and environmental issues. Drawing is an emphasis during this course and computer technology is incorporated as a major component of the animation process.
ANIMATION II202728.5 Credit      1.0 WeightPrerequisite(s): Satisfactory completion of Animation I

This course continues to explore the Animation Pathway by building upon both previously learned 3-D and 2-D traditional/digital animation techniques. Projects become more student centered, collaborative, and continue to be thematic in nature by investigating personal, social, and environmental issues. Projects become more student centered, collaborative, and continue to be thematic in nature by investigating personal, social, and environmental issues. Drawing is an emphasis during this course and computer technology is incorporated as a major component of the animation process. The next step for a student wishing to continue on this Visual Arts Pathway is to enroll in the Independent Studio, L3 course.
DESIGN AND DIGITAL MEDIA II 202652.5 Credit      1.0 WeightPrerequisite(s):  Satisfactory completion of Design & Digital Media I

This course continues to explore the Design and Digital Media Pathway by building upon previously learned computer functions, equipment, language, and programs. The emphasis continues to be on developing the students understanding and use of computer technologies as a design tool. Original drawings, paintings, and digital imagery continue to be imported and manipulated while exploring more complex individual ideas, opinions, and concepts. The next step for a student wishing to continue on this Visual Arts Pathways is to enroll in the Independent Studio, L3 course.
CERAMICS I202750.5 Credit      1.0 Weight

This is the introductory course to the Ceramics Visual
Arts Pathway. This course develops basic
hand building, wheel throwing, and sculpture skills. Instruction is given in the elements of
visual expression such as line, shape/form, space, texture, color, and value as
it applies to 3-D design. An
introduction to mold making, glazing, staining, and firing with emphasis on
developing and analyzing the creative process occurs.
CERAMICS II 202751.5 Credit      1.0 WeightPrerequisite(s): Satisfactory completion of Ceramics I

This course continues to explore the Ceramics Visual Arts Pathway. The first marking period students will build upon prior knowledge of basic hand building, wheel throwing techniques and surface treatments. The second marking period students get to choose to place their concentration on either hand building or throwing or a combination of the two. The next step for a student wishing to continue on this Visual Arts Pathway is to enroll in the Independent Studio, L4 course.

This course continues to explore the Sculpture Visual Arts Pathway by enhancing the skills and processes learned in Sculpture I. Indoor and outdoor sculpture are explored in a variety of materials and processes. Creative growth and problem solving are encouraged in the development of ideas and projects. The next step for a student wishing to continue on this Visual Arts Pathway is to enroll in the Independent Studio, L4 course.

This
course can be viewed as a 3rd level in the Drawing & Painting, Animation or Design & Digital Media Pathways. This course is student centered and each individual will collaborate with their instructor and peers to develop meaningful projects that help investigate and develop their 2-D and Digital artist process. Students will learn to identify and address their artistic strengths and weaknesses while continuing to explore higher order concepts, materials and media in alignment with their individual Visual Arts Pathways. The next step for a student wishing to continue on this Visual Arts Pathways is to enroll in the yearlong Honors Studio course.
HONORS ART STUDIO2028501 Credit      1.03 WeightPrerequisite(s): Satisfactory completion in either of the Independent Studio courses and Teacher Recommendation

This advanced course focuses on several areas of study in the 2-D, 3-D and Digital Arts. Development of a portfolio reflecting the individual students’ interests and artistic strength is a requirement of the course. In collaboration with the instructor(s) and peers, each student is expected to generate their own subject matter and demonstrate creative and analytical thinking. This course is designed for both the college bound student who wishes to major in the arts and the student who wishes to engage their art process and thinking at a higher level. The portfolio developed in this course will satisfy all requirements for college acceptance and once accepted student will continue to create works that align with the art scholarship process.
ART HISTORY ADVANCED PLACEMENT (SEMESTER) 202855.5 Credit      1.06 WeightAP Prerequisite(s): Satisfactory completion of English II Level I and Teacher Recommendation

The student gains an understanding and knowledge in architecture, sculpture, painting, and other art forms within diverse historical and cultural contexts. Students are evaluated through written essays, research projects and papers, and objective tests. Art History Advanced Placement is designed to prepare the student for taking the Advanced Placement test in Art History.

Business and Marketing Department courses should be considered as electives by all students. The Business and Marketing Department concentrates its offerings in three primary directions:   
All students can benefit from any courses in the Business and Marketing Department.
Developing saleable skills of the vocationally oriented student in the areas of administrative responsibilities, accounting, retail selling, banking, and other office duties.
Making available for all students the personal business courses that will enable them to solve consumer and legal problems they face now and will meet later in life.
Providing computer application courses and computer programming courses that prepare students to use technology tools to solve problems and enhance creative thinking.

INTRODUCTION TO BUSINESS AND ENTREPRENEURSHIP333001.5 Credit      1.0 WeightThis course allows students to discover how a business works and how it impacts lives on a daily basis. Topics presented include entrepreneurship, types of business ownership, marketing, promotion, management, and financing your business. Students will go through a step-by-step process of creating a business and developing a business plan. This course is an excellent introductory course for students interested in a business career.
ACCOUNTING333020.5 Credit      1.0 WeightPrerequisite(s):  Satisfactory Completion of Algebra I

Accounting is designed to build a foundation in accounting principles and procedures with emphasis being placed on developing habits of accuracy, legibility, promptness, and thoroughness. Accounting will also provide an introduction to automated accounting techniques. This course provides enough information so the student will be able to analyze and interpret the financial operations of a business. All students who plan to study Business Administration Management or Marketing in college should definitely choose Accounting as an elective.

PERSONAL FINANCE333083.5 Credit      1.0 WeightThis class is required in Grade 11 for all students.The goal for the students taking this course is to develop the ability to use knowledge and skills to manage their financial resources effectively for a lifetime of financial well-being. Students will cover topics including:  paychecks, taxes, financial institutions, budgeting, checking accounts, savings tools, investing, credit cards, credit reports, and insurance. These topics will be applied through project-based learning.
PERSONAL FINANCE (ONLINE OPTION)333080(OL).5 Credit      1.0 Weight
Students may elect to take the required Personal Finance class as an online course in Grade 11.

COLLEGE INTRODUCTION TO BUSINESS 3330111 Credit      1.06 WeightDual EnrollmentThis Penn College course provides the student with an introduction to a variety of business concepts and practices that impact all organizations, as well as knowledge and skills needed to be successful in an organization. Topics include interpersonal communications, emotional intelligence, economics, accounting, and finance and investments. An integrative approach connects topics and provides context within organizational environments, relevance to current business situations, and advances across various fields of business. There is no credit fee for this college course. Students are responsible for a book fee. The course is taught at both high schools. College in the high school courses are typically scheduled for students to attend in-person only Monday/Wednesday/Thursday. Staff hold open office hours on Tuesday/Friday for student support.

This Penn
College course provides an examination of the strategic use of social media for personal, professional, and business communication, advertising, and marketing. Course work includes using various social media tools, creating and sharing content, and collaborating on group campaigns using social media for social change. Includes analysis of current and emerging social media tools from a personal and business perspective. There is no credit fee for this college course. Students are responsible for a book fee. Students must have access to a smart phone (Android or Apple) and must include a data plan with Wi-Fi in order to access various social media platforms, such as Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat etc. College in the high school courses are typically scheduled for students to attend in-person only Monday/Wednesday/Thursday. Staff hold open office hours on Tuesday/Friday for student support.

Microsoft Office is a course designed to prepare students for computing efficiency that is essential for college and in the workplace. This course focuses on Microsoft Word, Excel and PowerPoint and prepares students for the Microsoft Office Specialist (MOS) exams in Word and PowerPoint. Microsoft Office certification can improve skills and distinguish students in today’s competitive academic environments. The MOS exams are an optional part of the course for a fee of approximately $90 to be paid by the student. Successful completion of the exams will provide a professional credential recognized around the world. This course will also help students earn cost-effective college credit
IT ESSENTIALS I: PC HARDWARE & SOFTWARE606150.5 Credit      1.0 Weight

This course introduces students to information technology and data communications. Students develop the necessary skills to enter this field by building a computer, installing the operating system, adding peripherals, connecting the computer to a local area network and to the Internet. This hands-on, lab-oriented course stresses laboratory safety and working effectively in a group environment. At the end of this course, students should possess the academic information and experience necessary to pass Comp TIA’s A+ certification. This certification is to test the student’s knowledge of learning objectives for a career as a Computer Repair Technician. The course is offered to students from both high schools but is taught at Cedar Cliff.
BASIC PROGRAMMING & DEVELOPING CODE606157.5 Credit      1.0 WeightNCAAPrerequisite(s): AlgebraThis course may be used to satisfy .5 graduation credits in Mathematics

This course will explore several common computer-programming languages such as Java, C, C++, Python and HTML.The content will prepare students with a
foundation for growth in a career, trade school, two-year or four-year program
for continuing education. Students will
be expected to learn languages and develop basic programs, applications, and
games.

COLLEGE COMPUTER APPLICATIONS3336761 Credit      1.06 WeightPrerequisite(s):  Students must enroll at Harrisburg Area Community College (HACC) and pay a per credit tuition. There is a HACC Bookstore charge added to the course which covers the cost of SimNet and includes an e-text.This course may be used to satisfy .5 graduation credits in ScienceThis HACC course provides a fundamental understanding of computers and familiarizes students with the interaction of computer hardware and software. Emphasis is on the application of microcomputers and “hands-on” use of software applications, including Microsoft Word, Excel and Access. This is one college-computing course; CIS105 as listed in the HACC course catalog. Upon the successful completion of the course, students will receive three (3) transferable HACC credits. Students must pass the reading and writing AccuPlacer test administered by HACC prior to enrolling in this course. The course is offered to students from both high schools but is taught at Red Land. College in the high school courses are typically scheduled for students to attend in-person only Monday/Wednesday/Thursday. Staff hold open office hours on Tuesday/Friday for student support.
NETWORKING I: NETWORKING FOR HOME & SMALL BUSINESSES 606111                 .5 Credit      1.0 Weight

This course teaches students the skills needed to obtain entry-level home network installer jobs. It also helps students develop some of the skills needed to become network technicians, computer technicians, cable installers, and help desk technicians. It provides a hands-on introduction to networking and the Internet using tools and hardware commonly found in home and small business environments. Instructors are encouraged to facilitate field trips and outside-the-classroom learning experiences. Labs include PC installation of game consoles, scanners, and cameras. The course is offered to students from both high schools but is taught at Cedar Cliff.
